summary: Patrick McKenzie shares practical strategies for running a software business alongside a demanding full-time job as a Japanese salaryman, drawing on four years of experience with Bingo Card Creator. He introduces the framework of 'time assets' versus 'time debt' as the key mental model for part-time entrepreneurs, arguing that building systems, processes, and automation is far more valuable than squeezing more hours out of each day. The post covers scope management, freelancer relationships, avoiding deadlines, documentation, and choosing problems suited to part-time constraints. McKenzie emphasizes that code is only about 10% of a software business, with marketing and operations consuming the rest.
key_points: Time can be 'stored' through building assets like code, documentation, and systems that save future time, rather than trying to squeeze more out of each day
Code represents roughly 10% of a software business — marketing, SEO, copywriting, and customer support dominate the effort and results
Every line of code is time debt that must be debugged, supported, documented, and potentially rewritten, so cutting scope aggressively is essential
Publicly committing to deadlines or feature schedules creates avoidable time debt and should be replaced with noncommittal acknowledgments
Build repeatable processes instead of chasing one-off events — a system for contacting small blogs beats wooing one large blog
money_quotes: The key insight about time is that software lets us take the old saying about how 'Everyone gets the same 24 hours per day' and break it open like a pinata.
Every line of code you write is time debt: it is another line that has to be debugged, another line that has to be supported, another line that may require a rewrite later.
People don't buy software because of what it does, they buy it for the positive change it will make in the life.
I snort in the general direction of anyone saying a nine-to-five job is impossible to juggle with a business because 'businesses require 100% concentration'.
Bingo Card Creator has been memorably described as 'Hello World attached to a random number generator.' If anything, that probably overstates its complexity.
There is no magic 'completion' day on an engineer's schedule: 'complete' is 100% a marketing decision that the software as it exists is Good Enough.
I started my business with $60. Literally.
Always, always, always trust the data over me if the data says I'm wrong. That's the easy part. The hard part is trusting the data when it is overruling you.
key_insight: Part-time entrepreneurs should focus on building 'time assets' — reusable systems, processes, and automation — rather than working more hours, because stored time compounds while spent time rots.
